name: Anaphylaxis
presentation: Rapid onset (seconds–minutes) after trigger exposure (food, drug, insect sting)
Urticaria and/or angioedema — erythematous wheals, swelling of lips/tongue/throat
Airway compromise — stridor (upper), wheeze (lower), sensation of throat tightening
GI symptoms — nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps (esp. food allergy)
signs: Angioedema — non-pitting swelling of face, lips, tongue, uvula
Stridor — inspiratory noise indicating upper airway obstruction

bedside: Obs: continuous BP, HR, O2 sats, RR — clinical diagnosis; do not delay treatment for tests
Peak flow if able — bronchospasm severity
ECG — exclude arrhythmia if cardiovascular compromise
bloods: Mast cell tryptase (MCT): 3 samples — at presentation (0 h), 1–2 h post-reaction, and convalescent (>24 h or follow-up); rise confirms mast cell degranulation
FBC — eosinophilia may support allergic diathesis (not acute diagnostic)
U&E/glucose — baseline; hypoglycaemia as alternative cause of collapse
imaging: CXR — if aspiration suspected, significant bronchospasm, or failure to respond to treatment
Limited acute role — clinical diagnosis drives immediate management

mx: IM adrenaline 500 mcg (0.5 ml of 1:1,000) anterolateral thigh — adults and children >12 yrs; repeat every 5 min if no improvement (paediatric: <6mo 100–150 mcg, 6mo–6yr 150 mcg, 6–12yr 300 mcg)
High-flow O2 + IV crystalloid for hypotension; lie flat with legs raised (never sit up a shocked patient)
Non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. loratadine) for persisting urticaria/angioedema after stabilisation — NOT as substitute for adrenaline; IV hydrocortisone removed from 2021 Resus Council guidelines
traps: Dose: 0.5 ml of 1:1,000 for adults — 1:10,000 is for IV use in cardiac arrest; using the wrong concentration underdoses by 10-fold
Adrenaline must be IM (anterolateral thigh), NOT IV — IV adrenaline outside resus causes fatal arrhythmias
Antihistamines and steroids do NOT treat anaphylaxis acutely — adrenaline is the only life-saving drug; hydrocortisone removed from 2021 guidelines entirely
Sitting up a shocked patient can precipitate cardiac arrest — lie flat + legs raised unless airway compromise forces upright

Biphasic reaction in up to 20%: observe ≥6 h after single dose, ≥12 h after 2 doses; prescribe 2 adrenaline auto-injectors on discharge + allergy clinic referral; tryptase elevated up to 12 h post-reaction
